response:
The fare calculation follows a clear rule where the base cost is ten yuan for any trip up to four kilometers. When the distance exceeds this limit the driver charges two yuan for each extra kilometer after rounding up any fraction. This pricing structure ensures that every additional kilometer adds a fixed amount to the total bill. The relationship between the fare and the distance can be expressed as a simple mathematical function for distances over four units. Such a system helps passengers understand their costs before the spring season brings more travelers to the city.

If a passenger paid thirty-eight yuan for a fifteen kilometer trip we must calculate the extra cost first. The base fare is ten yuan so the remaining twenty-eight yuan comes from the distance charges. Dividing twenty-eight by the rate of two yuan per kilometer gives us fourteen extra kilometers beyond the initial four. This means the total calculated distance was eighteen kilometers which implies three kilometers of added distance from waiting time. Since every five minutes of stopping equals one kilometer the taxi stopped for at most fifteen minutes. The driver likely waited during the busy spring rush when traffic slows down significantly near the hotel. This calculation shows how waiting time directly increases the final fare for the passenger. The city rules ensure that drivers are compensated for time spent idle on the road.
